title: Someone was in your server
problem: A colleague messages you — did you create a user called sysbackup? I did not touch it. You check and something feels very wrong. Investigate the server, find everything the attacker did, and lock it down before they come back.

setup.description: Plant evidence of a simulated breach. Run as root or with sudo.
setup.note: This creates a fake attacker user with planted history, a backdoor cron job and a malicious script in /tmp. Nothing actually calls out to the internet — the URLs are fake.

hints: Start with who is on this server — cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin will show real shell users, then check sudo group members with getent group sudo
Check auth logs to see when sysbackup was created and if it logged in — grep sysbackup /var/log/auth.log on Ubuntu or /var/log/secure on CentOS
Attackers always leave crons and files behind — check crontab -l -u sysbackup and hunt for recently modified files with find /tmp /var/tmp -mtime -7 -type f 2>/dev/null
solution:
1. Find suspicious users:
   cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin
   # Show users with real shells.
2. Check sudo members:
   getent group sudo
   # sysbackup should not be here.
3. Check login history:
   last sysbackup
   # See if and when they logged in.
4. Check auth logs (Ubuntu):
   grep sysbackup /var/log/auth.log
   # When was the account created.
5. Check auth logs (CentOS):
   grep sysbackup /var/log/secure
   # Same as above but the CentOS log path.
6. Read their history:
   sudo cat /home/sysbackup/.bash_history
   # What commands did they run.
7. Check their cron:
   sudo crontab -l -u sysbackup
   # Look for backdoor cron jobs.
8. Hunt planted files:
   find /tmp /var/tmp -mtime -7 -type f 2>/dev/null
   # Recently modified files in common attacker drop zones.
9. Read the script:
   cat /tmp/.rootkit.sh
   # Understand what it does before deleting.
10. Delete the script:
   sudo rm /tmp/.rootkit.sh
   # Remove the backdoor.
11. Remove sudo access:
   sudo gpasswd -d sysbackup sudo
   # Strip elevated privileges.
12. Lock the account:
   sudo usermod -L sysbackup
   # Disable login immediately.
13. Expire the account:
   sudo chage -E 0 sysbackup
   # Force account expiry.
14. Delete entirely:
   sudo userdel -r sysbackup
   # Remove user and home directory.
15. Verify clean:
   getent group sudo && last sysbackup
   # Confirm sudo is clean and no new logins.
